title: Source lineage for The Early South-English Legendary: Laud Misc. 108 Selection
summary: The checked-in aha2708 XML is a copy of University of Michigan item AHA2708.0001.001, a 2006 uncorrected OCR-derived Level 1 encoding of Carl Horstmann's 1887 EETS OS 87 edition. The encoded medieval subject is a sixty-three-component selection from the composite Laud Misc. 108: sixty-one components in the main hand-A legendary at ff. 23r–198r and two later hand-D lives at ff. 228v–233v. Horstmann's text is Laud-based but not sole-witness: Vernon supplies most of Dunstan 107–160 and Augustine 1–36, Harley 2277 supplies two Dunstan lines plus long Nicholas and Edmund of Abingdon lacunae, and Cotton Julius D IX supplies Eustace 86–179; named and abbreviated variant readings document wider collation. The XML's sixty DIV2 containers resolve to sixty-three intellectual components through repertory boundaries and one two-division merge. A complete 564-page scan of Horstmann, the complete 496-canvas Digital Bodleian Laud facsimile, and the complete 710-canvas Vernon facsimile are cached with exact work portions. Every materially used public source file—including catalog sidecars, DIMEV data, Bell/Couch scholarship, and Taylor's dissertation—is cached, fixity-recorded, and modeled separately from the target text and from its rights layer.
review_notes: The cataloging subject is the exact Horstmann/U-M edited selection, not a single homogeneous medieval work, the entire South English Legendary tradition, or the whole Laud codex.
The main and appendix target loci are discontiguous; f. 198r and f. 233v are shared boundary surfaces and are not evidence that intervening or following codex material belongs to the target.

Horstmann's Laud-base relation coexists with explicit transcribes relations for lacuna supplies from Vernon, Harley, and Cotton. Calling the edition sole-witness or diplomatic would erase material source layers.
The sixty-three component count is intellectual and repertorial, not a raw XML division count. The XML itself remains unchanged.
Complete IIIF means every canvas published by the provider at the selected local profile, not a preservation master. Caching records access and fixity, not permission to redistribute.

Date, dialect, codex production, and tradition-level compilation claims remain separated. The proposed Worcester origin is not projected onto Laud's possible Oxford production or its West Oxfordshire scribal language.

entities.label: General Corpus source XML for aha2708
entities.description: The exact XML artifact consumed by this repository's publication workflow and supplied through the pinned Kyvero-Press/CME git submodule.

entities.label: University of Michigan CME item AHA2708.0001.001
entities.description: A 2006 uncorrected OCR-derived TEI-in-Libraries Level 1 electronic text encoding Horstmann's title page, contents, Laud-based medieval text, pagination, notes, and omission placeholders, with linked page images in the U-M service.
notes: The encoding is explicitly uncorrected OCR with no editing or correction beyond basic structural markup; it is not a new manuscript transcription.
The text omits Horstmann's preliminary pages vii–l while retaining page-break scaffolding.

entities.label: Carl Horstmann, The Early South-English Legendary (1887)
entities.description: The immediate printed source of the U-M encoding. Horstmann uses Laud Misc. 108 as principal running-text witness, supplies defined lacunae from Vernon, Harley 2277, and Cotton Julius D IX, and prints a substantial variant apparatus.

bibliographic.citation: Carl Horstmann, ed., The Early South-English Legendary; or, Lives of Saints. I. MS. Laud, 108, in the Bodleian Library, Early English Text Society, Original Series 87 (London: N. Trübner & Co., 1887).
bibliographic.title: The Early South-English Legendary; or, Lives of Saints. I. MS. Laud, 108, in the Bodleian Library
bibliographic.series: Early English Text Society, Original Series 87

notes: Contemporary EETS matter projected a broader standard collection, additions, glossary, and source study; the title's 'I' does not by itself establish a missing narrative continuation to this exact Laud text.
Bibliographic records state that no further volume of this title was published.

entities.label: Internet Archive complete scan of Horstmann 1887
entities.description: A complete 564-page PDF carrier: outer EETS notices at PDF 1–12, title and contents at 13–16, printed preliminaries vii–l at 17–60, printed pp. 1–496 at 61–556, and trailing carrier matter at 557–564.
entities.reproduction_format: Complete page-image PDF with OCR derivatives and item metadata

entities.label: Oxford, Bodleian Library, MS Laud Misc. 108
entities.description: Composite parchment codex. The target is discontiguous: the main hand-A legendary selection occupies ff. 23r–198r and later hand-D Saints Blaise and Cecilia ff. 228v–233v. The target excludes ff. 1–22, intervening ff. 198r–228r, and the material following the shared f. 233v boundary.

date_statements.label: containing composite codex
date_statements.value: Late thirteenth to fourteenth century, with fourteenth-century additions

date_statements.label: target main hand A
date_statements.value: Late thirteenth or early fourteenth century

date_statements.label: target appendix hand D
date_statements.value: Middle or third quarter of the fourteenth century

holding.notes: Target main ff. 23r–198r and appendix ff. 228v–233v; f. 198r and f. 233v are shared textual boundary surfaces.

physical_description.extent: ii modern paper + i medieval parchment + 240/243 leaves; modern foliation 1–239 with 170 repeated
physical_description.format: Composite codex with target copied principally by hand A and later appendix by hand D
notes: Hand A's scribal dialect is associated with West Oxfordshire; hand D's language with western England, perhaps Gloucestershire, overlaid by East Anglian forms.
Latin and Anglo-Norman rubrics occur in the target. Possible Oxford production is tentative and is not a work-composition claim.

entities.label: Digital Bodleian complete facsimile of MS Laud Misc. 108
entities.description: The complete 496-canvas provider sequence, cached at the 1600-pixel IIIF image profile. Target main ff. 23r–198r are canvases 55–411; the later appendix ff. 228v–233v is canvases 472–482.
entities.reproduction_format: 496 color IIIF canvases at local 1600-pixel profile
notes: Complete means the complete sequence published by Digital Bodleian at the selected profile, not a preservation master or a grant of unrestricted reuse.

entities.label: Oxford, Bodleian Library, MS Eng. poet. a. 1 (Vernon Manuscript)
entities.description: Collateral and supply witness for Horstmann. Vernon supplies most of Horstmann's Dunstan lines 107–160 and Augustine lines 1–36, except Dunstan lines 143–144 supplied from Harley; DIMEV places the broad source loci at Dunstan f. 30vb–31rb and Augustine f. 31va–vb.

entities.label: Digital Bodleian complete facsimile of the Vernon Manuscript
entities.description: The complete 710-canvas provider sequence. The broad Dunstan/Augustine supply locus is mapped to ff. 30v–31v, canvases 80–82.
entities.reproduction_format: 710 color IIIF canvases at local 1600-pixel profile

entities.label: British Library Harley MS 2277
entities.description: Supply and collation witness. Horstmann uses it for Dunstan lines 143–144, Nicholas lines 361–448, and Edmund of Abingdon lines 224–313; DIMEV gives broad loci of Dunstan ff. 51–54, Nicholas ff. 177–182, and Edmund ff. 155–162.

entities.label: British Library Cotton MS Julius D IX
entities.description: Supply witness for Eustace lines 86–179. Horstmann rejects the more abridged Ashmole copy for this gap; DIMEV gives Cotton's broad Eustace locus as ff. 170–173v.

date_statements.label: manuscript date
date_statements.value: First half of the fifteenth century

entities.label: Oxford, Bodleian Library, MS Ashmole 43
entities.description: Named collateral witness in Horstmann's apparatus for selected readings in Francis, Brendan, Bartholomew, Eustace, and Edmund of Abingdon; it is not the running-text source for aha2708.

date_statements.label: manuscript date
date_statements.value: First quarter of the fourteenth century

entities.label: DIMEV version 1.0 records for the aha2708 components
entities.description: Machine-readable scholarly repertory data for the sixty-three intellectual components and their manuscript occurrences, used to distinguish work boundaries from the XML's sixty DIV2 containers.

entities.label: Bell and Couch, The Texts and Contexts of Oxford, Bodleian Library, MS Laud Misc. 108 (2011)
entities.description: Open-access scholarly book describing the composite codex, its hands, contents, literary contexts, and the layered South English Legendary tradition. It is supporting scholarship and does not reproduce the target medieval selection as an edition or facsimile.

bibliographic.citation: Kimberly K. Bell and Julie Nelson Couch, eds., The Texts and Contexts of Oxford, Bodleian Library, MS Laud Misc. 108: The Shaping of English Vernacular Narrative (Leiden: Brill, 2011).
bibliographic.title: The Texts and Contexts of Oxford, Bodleian Library, MS Laud Misc. 108: The Shaping of English Vernacular Narrative

entities.label: Tristan B. Taylor's 2023 study of Thomas Becket in the South English Legendaries
entities.description: Modern dissertation supporting the Becket component's genre, sources, and reception. It is not evidence for every component and does not contain the complete aha2708 target selection.

bibliographic.citation: Tristan B. Taylor, Thomas Becket in the South English Legendaries: Genre, Materiality, and Why the Reader Matters (PhD dissertation, University of Saskatchewan, 2023).
bibliographic.title: Thomas Becket in the South English Legendaries: Genre, Materiality, and Why the Reader Matters

rights.component: Repository XML file as distributed in the pinned CME submodule
rights.basis: The CME repository carries the Unlicense, phrased for software, but no item-specific statement was found conclusively applying that grant to imported U-M XML markup and text. The medieval and 1887 textual layers are addressed separately.

rights.component: U-M electronic text and item materials
rights.basis: The current U-M item page marks the materials public domain and exposes a No Copyright—United States rights statement. This does not govern Bodleian manuscript photographs.

rights.component: 1887 edition text, apparatus, and public-domain editorial matter
rights.basis: The edition was first published in 1887 and is outside the U.S. copyright term. This conclusion does not automatically cover modern scans or interfaces.

rights.component: Internet Archive scan, metadata, and derivative files as distinct digital layers
rights.basis: The underlying 1887 edition is public domain, but this record does not treat open access alone as an affirmative license covering every modern scan, metadata, or derivative layer.

rights.component: Underlying anonymous medieval verbal texts represented by Horstmann's 1887 publication
rights.jurisdiction: United States and jurisdictions where medieval copyright terms have expired
rights.basis: The anonymous medieval texts and their 1887 publication predate modern copyright terms by centuries; this does not cover modern photographs, catalog records, editions, or interfaces.

rights.component: Digital Bodleian manuscript photographs and IIIF image service

editorial_practices.source_feature: Composite Laud Misc. 108 contains legendary material at ff. 23r–198r and later ff. 228v–233v among other texts
editorial_practices.representation: Horstmann prints the main legendary sequence as pp. 1–483 and Saints Blaise and Cecilia as an appendix pp. 485–496
editorial_practices.description: The edition-defined target is discontiguous within the codex and does not represent the complete manuscript.

editorial_practices.source_feature: Laud contains lacunae and belongs to a large, variable South English Legendary tradition
editorial_practices.representation: Laud remains the principal running-text witness; Vernon, Harley 2277, and Cotton Julius D IX supply explicit lacunae, while named and abbreviated variant readings populate the apparatus
editorial_practices.description: The edition is not a sole-witness diplomatic transcription. An audit found 596 'al.' notes across forty-five XML divisions plus named Ashmole, Harley, Vernon, Cotton, Lambeth, and Trinity references.
editorial_practices.scope: Medieval text and apparatus, printed pp. 1–496
notes: The complete identity of every witness behind abbreviated 'al.' readings remains unresolved.

editorial_practices.source_feature: Horstmann's complete volume includes outer EETS matter, title, contents, fifty pages of preliminaries, medieval text, and apparatus
editorial_practices.representation: The U-M item encodes the title, contents, medieval text and notes, but omits the textual preliminaries vii–l while retaining PB scaffolding; the repository XML is explicitly uncorrected OCR and Level 1 structural markup
editorial_practices.description: The digital carrier is a selected, basic encoding of the edition rather than a complete or corrected scholarly re-edition.

editorial_practices.source_feature: The printed and repertory structure contains sixty-three intellectual components, including boundaries that do not align one-to-one with printed/XML divisions
editorial_practices.representation: The XML uses sixty DIV2 containers; the manifest resolves separately indexed Becket, prologue/Fabian-Sebastian, Julian, Michael, and Philip/James boundaries without changing the XML
editorial_practices.description: Raw XML division counts are not promoted into intellectual-work counts.

evidence.citation: General Corpus pinned CME source XML, CME/source/CME_phase_1-2/aha2708.xml at CME commit e1d77c88ca561be924b2fedc54dd474be2e7e49c.
evidence.locator: Complete file: HEADER, FRONT, title page, contents, omitted-preliminaries scaffold, BODY/DIV1[1–2], notes, milestones, and PB elements
evidence.summary: Exact evidence for artifact fixity, AHA2708.0001.001, the 2006 uncorrected OCR and Level 1 encoding statements, Horstmann title and pagination, the encoded selection, notes, and omission boundary.

evidence.locator: XPath/count audit of all DIV1, DIV2, HEAD, LG, L, NOTE1, P, PB, and MILESTONE nodes; comparison of boundary lines with DIMEV and Horstmann
evidence.summary: Found four DIV1, sixty DIV2, 67 HEAD, 146 LG, 16,877 nonempty BODY L, 1,526 NOTE1, four P outside BODY prose, 544 PB, and 358 MILESTONE. No L occurs in NOTE1 and no prose P occurs in BODY. Reconciled sixty XML divisions to sixty-three intellectual components and mapped title/contents, omitted vii–l, and printed pp. 1–496.

evidence.citation: University of Michigan Library, Corpus of Middle English Prose and Verse, item AHA2708.0001.001.
evidence.locator: item header, bibliographic information, full-text navigation, publication statement, and rights link
evidence.summary: Current official item page confirming stable identifier, title, Horstmann's 1887 publication, 2006 electronic-text status, and a public-domain / No Copyright—United States statement.

evidence.citation: University of Michigan Library, About the Corpus of Middle English Prose and Verse.
evidence.summary: Collection-level explanation that CME electronic texts were rekeyed or derived from out-of-copyright historical editions and given basic markup; it names the Laud South English Legendary among the corpus holdings.

evidence.summary: The repository carries the Unlicense, phrased for software. No explicit statement was found extending it conclusively to every imported U-M XML layer, so artifact rights remain conservatively unknown.

evidence.citation: Carl Horstmann, ed., The Early South-English Legendary; or, Lives of Saints. I. MS. Laud, 108, in the Bodleian Library, EETS OS 87 (London: N. Trübner & Co., 1887).
evidence.locator: PDF 13–16 title/contents; 17–60 preliminaries vii–l; 61–556 printed pp. 1–496; especially preface pp. vii–x, contents, lacuna transition notes, and apparatus
evidence.summary: Complete primary edition evidence for bibliographic identity, the Laud-base editorial method, supplied Vernon/Harley/Cotton passages, variant collation, printed pagination, component boundaries, and the main/appendix distinction. qpdf validates all 564 pages.

evidence.locator: complete JSON response and file inventory

evidence.locator: complete codex description; target legendary contents ff. 23r–198r and ff. 228v–233v; hands, dates, dialects, rubrics, and provenance
evidence.summary: Current official evidence for shelfmark, historical designation, material and extent, target's discontiguous locus, scribal hands and dates, West Oxfordshire and western/Gloucestershire linguistic associations, Anglo-Norman and Latin rubrics, possible Oxford origin, and adjacent-text boundaries.

evidence.citation: Digital Bodleian complete IIIF object for MS Laud Misc. 108.

evidence.locator: complete codex description and South English Legendary contents
evidence.summary: Current official evidence for shelfmark, first-quarter-fourteenth-century date, physical context, and the collateral witness used in Horstmann's named variant notes.

evidence.citation: Digital Index of Middle English Verse, version 1.0 data release: sixty-three records representing the aha2708 components and their manuscript occurrences.
evidence.summary: Establishes sixty-three intellectual component identities, incipits/explicits, verse form, line counts, broad manuscript loci, and witness occurrence context. It also exposes the prologue count and Edward naming discrepancies recorded as open questions.
notes: The embedded licence is Creative Commons Attribution 4.0 International.
The complete supporting release contains repertory metadata and short quotations, not the complete target medieval selection.

evidence.citation: Kimberly K. Bell and Julie Nelson Couch, eds., The Texts and Contexts of Oxford, Bodleian Library, MS Laud Misc. 108: The Shaping of English Vernacular Narrative (Brill, 2011).
evidence.locator: introduction; A. S. G. Edwards, pp. 21–30; Thomas R. Liszka, pp. 31–50; chapters addressing Laud's legendary layers, hands, and contexts
evidence.summary: Supports the deliberate composite-anthology model, exact Laud contents and hand boundaries, layered rather than single-author legendary development, cautious chronology and geography, West Oxfordshire main scribal language, and the distinct later appendix. The complete supporting book does not reproduce the full target selection and carries CC BY-NC 4.0 notices.

evidence.citation: Tristan B. Taylor, Thomas Becket in the South English Legendaries: Genre, Materiality, and Why the Reader Matters (PhD dissertation, University of Saskatchewan, 2023).
evidence.locator: abstract and chapters on the Laud-family Becket Life and Translation
evidence.summary: Supports the Becket component's hybrid hagiographic, romance, and historiographical context and the distinction between Life and Translation. It is scoped to that component, remains copyrighted, and does not reproduce the complete aha2708 target.

evidence.citation: U.S. Copyright Office, Circular 15A, Duration of Copyright.
evidence.locator: works published before 1978 and public-domain term tables
evidence.summary: Official legal guidance supporting the U.S. public-domain conclusion for the 1887 published edition. It does not answer rights in modern photographs, encodings, or catalog data.

open_questions.question: What are the exact source-side subfolio endpoints for each continuous Vernon, Harley 2277, and Cotton Julius D IX passage supplied by Horstmann?
next_steps: Collate the exact Horstmann transition lines against full source images or authoritative transcriptions.
Preserve the established broad DIMEV loci until exact endpoints are proven.

open_questions.question: Which witnesses stand behind all 596 abbreviated 'al.' notes across forty-five XML divisions, and what is Horstmann's complete collation corpus?
next_steps: Reconstruct every abbreviation and named witness from the complete apparatus.
Do not treat the currently modeled named witnesses as exhaustive.

open_questions.question: Which exact physical or digital copy of Horstmann 1887 supplied U-M's page images and OCR?
next_steps: Ask U-M for digitization provenance or inspect METS/source metadata if made public.
Continue treating the Internet Archive PDF as a representative complete scan, not as the demonstrated parent of the U-M encoding.

open_questions.question: Can individual exemplars, poets, compilers, revision layers, and dates be assigned more securely across all sixty-three components?
next_steps: Review component-level source studies and the full witness tradition without projecting one origin or author across the anthology.

open_questions.question: How should three source discrepancies be resolved: prologue twenty-two versus DIMEV twenty-six lines, seven versus eight lost opening items, and Edward the Martyr versus catalog/DIMEV 'Edward the Elder'?
next_steps: Check the printed incipits/explicits, manuscript sequence, and current repertory/catalog editorial files.
Contact the relevant catalog or DIMEV editors if internal evidence cannot resolve the inherited labels.
